results.possibleIdentity: Mark Friedman was a researcher and engineer active in the late 1960s and 1970s, associated with the Institute of Animal Behaviour (IAB) at Rutgers University in Newark, New Jersey. His work spanned the multidisciplinary fields of bio-engineering, biology, psychology, and ultrasonics. Within the Institute of Animal Behaviour, he was involved in technical research that likely supported the study of animal communication and physiological psychology, specifically utilizing ultrasonics—a field focused on recording and analyzing high-frequency vocalizations in animals like rodents. Friedman is notably recognized for his contribution to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg to foster collaborations between artists and the technical community. He compiled 'Experiments in Art and Technology: A Bibliography of E.A.T. Materials,' published in 1972, which serves as a primary historical record of the organization's activities, projects, and member contributions during its formative years.
